theme.bgGradient: radial-gradient(1200px 700px at 12% -10%, rgba(56,189,248,.16), transparent 60%), radial-gradient(1000px 600px at 100% 0%, rgba(129,140,248,.16), transparent 55%), linear-gradient(160deg, #0A0F24, #111A3A 55%, #16224D)

title: Software for an Audience of One
description: Why the marginal cost of bespoke software has collapsed to near zero — inverting the economics that forced 'build for the mass' into 'build for this one person' — and where the cost of correctness relocates. Based on the 2026 essay by Satoshi Nakajima.

beats.text: Software for an audience of one. Why the marginal cost of bespoke software has collapsed to near zero — and what that new phase of software development costs.

slide.title: Software for an **Audience of One**
slide.subtitle: The marginal cost of bespoke software just collapsed to {primary:near zero.}
slide.author: Satoshi Nakajima

beats.text: Look at one real workspace today. Six collections have grown their own bespoke interfaces — each a self-contained HTML file the agent wrote on request, none of it ever seen by its owner.

slide.title: One workspace, six bespoke apps
slide.subtitle: Each a self-contained HTML view the agent wrote on request
rows: Tokyo restaurants as a subway map

callout.text: The owner never wrote — or even saw — a line of the HTML. He described what he wanted; the code appeared.
callout.label: Key fact

beats.text: Every one of these is software that no company would ever build. The addressable market for each is exactly one person — and under fifty years of software economics, that made them not just hard, but impossible.

slide.quote: Every one of these is software that {highlight:**no company would ever build.**} The addressable market is exactly one person.
slide.author: Not hard — *impossible*, under the old economics.
slide.role: Build cost far exceeded the value any single user could justify

beats.text: The old constraint was a cost structure. Building software was expensive and fixed; serving one more user was free. The rational strategy was to spread the fixed cost over as many users as possible — build for the mass.

slide.title: The economics that forced "software for the mass"
slide.subtitle: Individualization was never a preference problem — it was arithmetic

items: Regress to the mean of need

callout.text: The settings panel you never fully use; the feature "not worth building for your use case." Not a failure of taste — the arithmetic.
callout.label: Symptom

beats.text: The constraint had a single load-bearing term: building software is expensive. An LLM that generates working code on demand removes it — and the whole structure falls.

slide.title: One term removed, the whole structure falls

items.text: Build cost: large, fixed, up front

content.label: users needed to repay one build

items.text: Build cost ≈ a sentence of intent

content.label: user is now a viable audience

callout.text: When building for one is as cheap as building for millions, the reason to build for millions — {primary:cost amortization} — evaporates.
callout.label: The inversion

beats.text: This is not merely 'software is easier to build.' The category that was economically impossible for the entire history of the field — software whose audience is a single person — has become the default for a large class of needs.

slide.quote: The interesting unit of software is no longer the product shipped to a market. It is {primary:**the artifact grown for an individual.**}
slide.author: Not "software is easier to build" — that undersells it
slide.role: The impossible category is now the default

beats.text: The most important detail is the one easiest to skip: the owner never saw the HTML. When code costs almost nothing, it stops being the asset and becomes an intermediate representation between intent and behavior.

slide.title: Code becomes an intermediate representation
slide.subtitle: The durable things are the two ends; the code is the compile target between them

content.value: *"Draw my restaurants as a subway map."*
**Regrown on demand** — no more precious than compiler-emitted assembly.

callout.text: "Also color the stations by cuisine" opens no code review — it reissues the request. It is cheaper to {primary:regenerate} the artifact than to repair it.
callout.label: Maintenance

beats.text: A claim that LLMs invented software-for-one would be false. The category already existed — its name was the spreadsheet. What's new is that the LLM is the first tool to remove both of its constraints at once.

slide.title: Spreadsheets got there first — at a price
slide.subtitle: VisiCalc, HyperCard, Excel, no-code: bespoke software with a market of one

left.title: Spreadsheet / No-code

content.value: **Authoring surface:** constrained DSL — a grid or block palette.
**Authoring burden:** the *user* assembles it — must become, partly, a programmer.

left.footer: Removed the dev team, kept the user in a grid

content.value: **Authoring surface:** arbitrary code — full HTML/JS/charts. A subway-map layout no builder offers.
**Authoring burden:** the *agent* writes it — you don't assemble, you **describe**.

callout.text: Not a no-code platform handing the user blocks — an {primary:agent that materializes the surface on request.}
callout.label: The novelty

beats.text: But honesty requires naming what this gives up. Mass software is extraordinarily good at one thing: accumulating correctness. Millions of users debug it for you. Software for one has no such audience — its bugs ship to their only user.

items.text: Hardened by many eyes over years

right.title: Software for one

items.text: No other eyes will ever run it
Correct — or not — between you and the model, once
{danger:The bug ships to its only user: you}

right.footer: From the vendor's QA org → one user, one LLM

callout.text: The spreadsheet era's danger was never the visible UI — it was the {danger:invisible formula error} no one else ever checked. A wrong poster wall is harmless; a wrong portfolio allocation is not.
callout.label: Old lesson

beats.text: The mitigations are the boring, correct ones — and they are the real frontier work of this phase. The codegen is solved; verification is not.

slide.title: The real frontier work — not the codegen

items.title: Keep the data independently verifiable.
items.description: The chart can be wrong; the numbers it reads must not be. Durable data, disposable view.

items.title: Reserve review for consequential views.
items.description: Human or deterministic checks where the arithmetic matters; let harmless views run unaudited — the reliability dial.

items.title: Treat regenerability as a safety feature.
items.description: An artifact cheap to throw away is also cheap to distrust and replace.

items.title: Distrust beats defending precious code.
items.description: Institutions defend expensive code long past its correctness. Disposable code carries no such loyalty.

beats.text: Two sentences capture the change. The old phase: build once, for the mass, because building is expensive — the code is the asset. The new phase: build for one, on demand, because building is nearly free — intent and data are the asset.

items.text: Build **once, for the mass** — building is expensive
Build **for one, on demand** — building is nearly free
{success:Intent and data are the asset; code is disposable}

callout.text: From maintaining precious code → to {primary:verifying disposable code} and curating the durable intent and data behind it.
callout.label: Discipline shift

beats.text: A restaurant guide drawn as a subway map is not a toy at the edge of this shift — it is the shape of the whole thing: software no market would fund, built for one person who never saw the code, kept only as long as it is useful, and regenerated the moment it is not.

slide.quote: Software no market would fund, built for one person who never saw the code — kept while useful, {primary:**regenerated the moment it is not.**}
slide.author: *Software for an Audience of One* — Satoshi Nakajima, 2026
